title: podman system reset should reset the configuration for plugins it uses
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

Also, there is a running list of known issues in the [Podman Troubleshooting Guide](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/master/troubleshooting.md),
please reference that page before opening a new issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**
/kind feature

**Description**

In my attempts to migrate from docker to podman, I've run into several issues that left remnants in either iptables or the filesystem [e.g. see #10806, #10795, [containers/dnsname#54](https://github.com/containers/dnsname/issues/54)] and `podman system reset` was unable to properly clean those remnants up. From an end-user perspective, this is rather confusing since the docs are not particularly clear on the matter:
> *podman system reset reads the current configuration and attempts to remove all of the relevant configurations.*

What's a 'relevant' configuration? And if a configuration is not relevant (e.g. perhaps because it's managed by a CNI plugin a not podman itself), then what should users be doing to clean *that*?

IMO, `podman system reset` should either take responsibility for cleaning up after plugins it uses, or a cleanup hook should trigger those plugins to do the same when requested. This would ensure that the software reset behaves as expected from an end-user perspective, regardless of the particular plugin config.

#10806 is a particularly interesting case because `podman system reset` presently doesn't help for any iptables rules left behind - chain prefixes or comments should be used to track rules inserted by podman so that the system reset can take responsibility for them, even if they were unintentionally left behind.


**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. Use podman with docker-compose and run into some of the linked issues above
2. `podman system reset`

**Describe the results you received:**
port mapping or containers are still broken (until next reboot)

**Describe the results you expected:**
a clean system, including any iptables and CNI plugin configurations generated by podman plugins

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
Version:      3.2.2
API Version:  3.2.2
Go Version:   go1.16.4
Built:        Fri Jun 25 16:49:19 2021
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```

**Output of `podman info --debug`:**

comments.body:
If the CNI plugins offered a way to perform such a reset, we could utilize it; however, they don't, and it does not seem likely they will begin to do so.

This can be a goal for the network stack rework, though.
On firewalld systems you can run `firewall-cmd --reload`, this should clear all rules.
well, we could nuke all the cni configuration files ... but that won't help the iptables stuff.

> On firewalld systems you can run firewall-cmd --reload, this should clear all rules.

This is what I used to delete the lingering iptables rules - but this shouldn't be necessary from an end-user perspective. IMO, podman should know which rules it created and be able to clean them up without the user having to have knowledge of podman internals and how to reset the other components it has side-effects on.

> well, we could nuke all the cni configuration files ... but that won't help the iptables stuff.

Please do! I recognize this delineation between podman and CNI makes cleanup difficult but from an end-user/UX perspective, system reset should take care of it. A cleanup hook, or simply deleting those CNI folders if we can safely assume they're not used for anything else would be desired. Failing that, at least a warning printed to the user to go look there themselves.

For deleting CNI configuration files - I'm very reluctant because we don't know which ones Podman made. On a mixed-use system, this could cause `podman system reset` to wipe config files in use by CRI-O/Buildah or another CNI consumer, rendering them unable to start containers. Maybe we could only delete the ones with Podman-specific metadata fields added (labels, maybe) added?

> Maybe we could only delete the ones with Podman-specific metadata fields added (labels, maybe) added?

That would certainly help for cases like containers/dnsname#54 where a bug in the plugin leaves behind broken configs after podman execution!

@Matt Heon ***@***.***> I'm starting top think this feature would be
more implementable in the future when network stack changes have been made,
which is why I labelled it to Podman 4.

On Fri, Jul 9, 2021 at 12:56 PM Matthew Heon ***@***.***>
wrote:

> know which ones Podman made. On a mixed-use system, this could cause podman
> system reset to wipe config files in use by CRI-O/Buildah or another CNI
> consumer, rendering them unable to start containers. Maybe we could only
> delete the ones with Podman-specific metadata fields added (labels, maybe)
> added?

>
This is not fixed, we remove network configs but not iptables rules as requested

Thanks - glad to hear network configs are cleaned up. That one took the most time to debug / was the least obvious to me as a new user, as I wasn't familiar with where all the config internals were stored for various plugins.

The lingering iptables rules, while more obvious, caused port mapping breakage so will be great to have those fixed as well.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
@mheon @Luap99 is this possible to fix?
We can potentially make Netavark smart enough to clear all netavark-generated firewall rules; but I don't think we can fix for CNI.
I think we could implement something like this in netavark but I think we should rather use the time and fix actual problems which would cause the cleanup to fail.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
@Luap99 @mheon what's your take on this issue today?

I really don't think it is worth the effort. It is possible to implement it in netavark but in almost all cases podman will cleanup successfully so I do not see how thinks are leaking in normal uses (and if they are leaking we should prioritize fixing those).
OK, then let's close this issue. Thanks everybody!
